title: Polish pronunciation
summary: The sounds and letters that don't exist in English — plus the digraphs that trip everyone up.

phonemes.name: a-ogonek
phonemes.englishApproximation: Nasal 'o' — like the French 'on' in 'bon'.
phonemes.description: Nasal vowel. At word-end and before fricatives it stays a true nasal /ɔ̃/. Before stops it splits: /ɔm/ before p/b, /ɔn/ before t/d, /ɔŋ/ before k/g.

phonemes.name: e-ogonek
phonemes.englishApproximation: Nasal 'e' — like 'en' in French 'vin' but with an 'e' base.
phonemes.description: Nasal vowel. At word-end it usually denasalizes to plain /ɛ/ in casual speech. Before stops it splits the same way as ą (em / en / eŋ).

phonemes.name: o-kreska
phonemes.englishApproximation: Identical to 'u' — sounds like 'oo' in 'boot'.
phonemes.description: Sounds exactly like the letter u. The two spellings are historical and you just have to memorize which word uses which. Often ó alternates with o in related forms (stół → stoły).

phonemes.name: y
phonemes.englishApproximation: Like the 'i' in 'bit' but pulled further back in the mouth.
phonemes.description: A central vowel — not the English 'y'. Position your tongue between 'ee' and 'uh'. It's the default vowel after hard consonants (sz, cz, rz, ż, c, dz, r, ł).

phonemes.name: ł (l-with-stroke)
phonemes.englishApproximation: Like English 'w' in 'water'.
phonemes.description: Not an L sound at all — it's an English 'w'. A frequent stumbling block: 'było' is 'BIH-woh', not 'BIH-loh'. Historically it was a velarized L, preserved only by some older speakers.

phonemes.name: w
phonemes.englishApproximation: Like English 'v' in 'van'.
phonemes.description: Always a V sound — never the English 'w'. Devoices to /f/ at word-end or next to voiceless consonants (e.g. 'w' alone, used as a preposition before voiceless consonants).

phonemes.name: c
phonemes.englishApproximation: Like 'ts' in 'cats'.
phonemes.description: Always /ts/ — never the English 'k' or 's'. Before i it palatalizes (see ć).

phonemes.name: ch (= h)
phonemes.englishApproximation: Like the German 'ch' in 'Bach' — a raspy back-of-throat H.
phonemes.description: Identical in sound to the letter 'h'. A bit raspier than English H. The two spellings (ch vs h) are historical and must be memorized per word.

phonemes.name: r
phonemes.englishApproximation: A tapped/trilled R like Spanish or Italian — never the English R.
phonemes.description: A short alveolar trill: the tip of the tongue taps just behind the upper teeth. Most often a single tap; in careful or emphatic speech a full trill.

phonemes.name: ć / ci
phonemes.englishApproximation: A very soft, hissy 'ch' — like 'ch' in 'cheese' but with the tongue further forward and softer.
phonemes.description: The 'soft' version of cz. Written ć at end of words or before consonants; written 'ci' before a vowel (cia, cie, cio, ciu) and 'ci' alone when followed by another consonant.

phonemes.name: ś / si
phonemes.englishApproximation: A very soft, hissy 'sh' — like the 'sh' in 'sheep' but more whispery, tongue forward.
phonemes.description: The 'soft' version of sz. Written ś before consonants and at word-end; written 'si' before a vowel (sia, sie, sio, siu).

phonemes.name: ń / ni
phonemes.englishApproximation: Soft palatal 'n' — like Spanish 'ñ' in 'mañana'.
phonemes.description: The 'soft' version of n. Written ń before consonants and at word-end; written 'ni' before a vowel.

phonemes.name: ż (z-dot)
phonemes.englishApproximation: Like the 's' in 'measure' or French 'j' in 'jour' — but harder, with the tongue curled back.
phonemes.description: A retroflex 'zh'. Identical in sound to the digraph 'rz' (which you'll meet separately). The two spellings are historical.

phonemes.name: rz (= ż)
phonemes.englishApproximation: Same sound as ż — like 'zh' in 'measure', harder.
phonemes.description: Pronounced identically to ż. Devoices to /ʂ/ (like sz) when adjacent to voiceless consonants — e.g. 'przepraszam' starts with a /pʂ/. The spelling is preserved because it often alternates with r in related words (morze ↔ morski).

phonemes.name: ź / zi
phonemes.englishApproximation: A very soft 'zh' — like the 's' in 'vision' but more whispery, tongue forward.
phonemes.description: The 'soft' counterpart of ż. Written ź before consonants and at word-end; written 'zi' before a vowel (zia, zie, zio, ziu).

phonemes.name: sz
phonemes.englishApproximation: Like English 'sh' in 'ship' but harder, with the tongue curled back.
phonemes.description: The 'hard' sh. Tongue is pulled back and slightly curled. Followed by y (not i): szyba, szykować.

phonemes.name: cz
phonemes.englishApproximation: Like English 'ch' in 'church' but harder, with the tongue curled back.
phonemes.description: The 'hard' ch. Followed by y (not i): czysty, czyj. Compare with the much softer ć.

phonemes.name: dz
phonemes.englishApproximation: Like the 'ds' in 'beds' or 'kids'.
phonemes.description: A single voiced affricate — say it in one push, not 'd' + 'z'. The voiced counterpart of c.

phonemes.name: dź / dzi
phonemes.englishApproximation: A soft 'j' sound — like 'j' in 'jeans' but lighter, tongue further forward.
phonemes.description: The voiced counterpart of ć. Written dź before consonants and at word-end; written 'dzi' before a vowel.

phonemes.name: dż
phonemes.englishApproximation: Like English 'j' in 'jungle' — the hard, retroflex version.
phonemes.description: The voiced counterpart of cz. Mostly appears in loanwords (dżem, dżinsy) and a handful of native words (drożdże).
